paper_info.author: Paula Restrepo, BA; Sherry Bhalla, PhD; Yogita Ghodke-Puranik, PhD; Adolfo Aleman, MPA; Violetta Leshchenko, PhD; David T. Melnekoff, MS; Sarita Agte, MD; Joy Jiang, BS; Deepu Madduri, MD; Joshua Richter, MD; Shambavi Richard, MD; Ajai Chari, MD; Hearn Jay Cho, MD, PhD; Sundar Jagannath, MD; Christopher J. Walker, PhD; Yosef Landesman, PhD; Alessandro Laganà, PhD; and Samir Parekh, MD

evidence_items.evidence_description: A three-gene expression signature comprising WNT10A, DUSP1, and ETV7 predicts response to selinexor-based therapy in multiple myeloma patients. The signature was validated in the BOSTON cohort (n=100), STORM cohort of triple-class refractory MM patients (n=64), and an external real-world cohort (n=35). The signature tracks with both depth and duration of response and reveals upregulated interferon-mediated apoptotic signaling that may prime tumors to respond to selinexor-based therapy.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: The three-gene signature predicts response to selinexor-based therapy in patients with MM in the BOSTON cohort. Then, we validated this gene signature in 64 patients from the STORM cohort of triple-class refractory MM and additionally in an external cohort of 35 patients treated in a real-world setting outside of clinical trials. We found that the signature tracks with both depth and duration of response.
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable because the validated three-gene signature could guide treatment decisions for selinexor therapy in MM patients. The evidence is supported by validation across multiple independent cohorts including clinical trial and real-world settings. Consolidating as a unified signature better represents the biological reality that these genes function together as a predictive biomarker.
evidence_items.source_title: A Three-Gene Signature Predicts Response to Selinexor in Multiple Myeloma

clinical_trial_details.title: Bortezomib, Selinexor, and Dexamethasone in Patients With Multiple Myeloma

evidence_items.evidence_description: The three-gene expression signature (WNT10A, DUSP1, ETV7) originally developed for multiple myeloma also validates in recurrent glioblastoma patients treated with selinexor. The signature was evaluated using a cohort of pretreatment tumors from patients with recurrent glioblastoma, demonstrating cross-tumor-type predictive utility.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: We found that the signature tracks with both depth and duration of response, and it also validates in a different tumor type using a cohort of pretreatment tumors from patients with recurrent glioblastoma.
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable as the validated signature could potentially guide selinexor treatment decisions in glioblastoma patients. However, confidence is lower and evidence level is C because: (1) this is a secondary validation in a different tumor type, (2) specific cohort size not provided in abstract, (3) validation data appears less extensive than for MM. Full paper review would be needed for more details.
evidence_items.source_title: A Three-Gene Signature Predicts Response to Selinexor in Multiple Myeloma

clinical_trial_details.title: Study Evaluating the Efficacy and Safety of Selinexor (KPT-330) in Participants With Recurrent Gliomas

evidence_items.evidence_description: WNT10A expression is a component of a validated three-gene signature (WNT10A, DUSP1, ETV7) that predicts response to selinexor-based therapy in multiple myeloma. The signature was validated across BOSTON (n=100), STORM (n=64), and real-world (n=35) cohorts. WNT10A contributes to upregulated interferon-mediated apoptotic signaling associated with selinexor response.
evidence_items.verbatim_quote: Furthermore, the genes involved in the signature, WNT10A, DUSP1, and ETV7, reveal a potential mechanism through upregulated interferon-mediated apoptotic signaling that may prime tumors to respond to selinexor-based therapy.
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable as part of a multi-gene signature for treatment selection. Note: WNT10A should be evaluated in context of the complete three-gene signature, not independently. Expression directionality (high vs low) not explicitly stated in abstract - requires verification from full paper results.

evidence_items.evidence_description: ETV7 expression is a component of a validated three-gene signature (WNT10A, DUSP1, ETV7) that predicts response to selinexor-based therapy in multiple myeloma. The signature was validated across BOSTON (n=100), STORM (n=64), and real-world (n=35) cohorts. The signature has clinical relevance in identifying patients most likely to benefit from selinexor-based therapy.

This signature has important clinical relevance as it could identify patients with cancer who are most likely to benefit from treatment with selinexor-based therapy.
evidence_items.extraction_reasoning: This is actionable as part of a multi-gene signature for treatment selection. Note: ETV7 should be evaluated in context of the complete three-gene signature, not independently. Expression directionality (high vs low) not explicitly stated in abstract - requires verification from full paper results.

plan.key_variants: WNT10A expression, DUSP1 expression, ETV7 expression, three-gene signature (WNT10A+DUSP1+ETV7), XPO1 mutations, XPO1 C528 mutation
plan.key_diseases: Multiple Myeloma, Relapsed/Refractory Multiple Myeloma, Triple-class Refractory Multiple Myeloma
plan.focus_sections: RESULTS section (primary cohort analysis, validation cohorts), METHODS section (patient cohorts: BOSTON n=100, STORM n=64, real-world n=35), ABSTRACT (summary of predictive performance), TABLES (response rates, survival data, hazard ratios), FIGURES (survival curves, gene expression correlations)
plan.extraction_notes:
This paper presents a PREDICTIVE biomarker study for selinexor response in multiple myeloma. Focus on:

1. THREE-GENE SIGNATURE (WNT10A, DUSP1, ETV7): Extract evidence for the combined signature's ability to predict response to selinexor-based therapy. Look for hazard ratios, response rates, PFS/OS data stratified by signature status.

2. COHORTS: Three validation cohorts mentioned:
   - BOSTON study (n=100): Primary discovery cohort
   - STORM study (n=64): Triple-class refractory MM validation
   - Real-world cohort (n=35): External validation
   - Glioblastoma cohort (n=unknown): Cross-tumor validation

3. EVIDENCE TYPE: This is primarily PREDICTIVE evidence (biomarker predicts response to specific therapy). The signature predicts response specifically to selinexor-based therapy (not general prognostic).

4. KEY METRICS TO EXTRACT:
   - Response rates in signature-high vs signature-low patients
   - PFS/OS hazard ratios
   - Depth and duration of response correlations
   - Validation performance in each cohort

5. INDIVIDUAL GENES: May also extract evidence for WNT10A, DUSP1, and ETV7 individually if data supports their independent predictive value.

6. XPO1 MUTATIONS: Paper mentions XPO1 mutations as candidate biomarkers but states they were NOT significantly correlated with response. Extract this as negative evidence if explicitly stated.

7. MECHANISTIC CONTEXT: The genes are linked to interferon-mediated apoptotic signaling, but focus on clinical predictive value, not just mechanism.

CRITICAL: Ensure evidence items clearly distinguish this as PREDICTIVE (predicts response to selinexor) rather than PROGNOSTIC (general outcome regardless of therapy). The signature tracks with response specifically to selinexor-based therapy in XVd regimen vs standard Vd regimen.

final_critique.missing_items:
No critical missing items. The Extractor has addressed the glioblastoma evidence that was missing in iteration 1.

Optional considerations:
1. **XPO1 mutations negative evidence**: The paper metadata mentions "XPO1 mutations (mentioned as candidate biomarkers but not significantly correlated with response)" - if the full paper discusses this with statistics, a DOES_NOT_SUPPORT evidence item could be valuable, but this would require access to results beyond the abstract.

2. **Four-gene imputed activity signature from STORM**: The reader identified "IRF3, ARL2BP, ZBTB17, ATRX (four-gene imputed activity signature from STORM)" in the metadata. Without full paper access, cannot determine if this represents extractable evidence.

3. **Combination therapy specificity**: The abstract mentions "selinexor-based therapy" and metadata references "XVd (selinexor, bortezomib, dexamethasone combination)". Current items use "Selinexor" as therapy. Consider if combination vs monotherapy specificity is important, though "selinexor-based therapy" appears to be the accurate representation from the abstract.
final_critique.extra_items:
The extraction now contains both:
- A unified three-gene signature item (item #1 for MM, item #2 for glioblastoma)
- Three separate individual gene expression items (items #3-5 for WNT10A, DUSP1, ETV7)

**Assessment**: This represents potential redundancy but is NOT a critical flaw because:

1. The individual gene items explicitly state they are "components of a validated three-gene signature" and should not be evaluated independently
2. The unified signature items provide the primary clinical evidence
3. This structure may align with CIViC database requirements for multi-gene signatures

**Recommendation**: If CIViC database policy is to create separate entries for each gene in a multi-gene signature (with cross-references), then all 5 items are appropriate. If policy is to use only unified signature entries, then items #3-5 could be considered redundant.

Given the uncertainty about CIViC's preferred structure for multi-gene signatures and that the individual items appropriately contextualize themselves, I'm approving this approach. However, the submitter should verify CIViC's guidelines for multi-gene biomarkers before final submission.
final_critique.summary:
**ITERATION 2 ASSESSMENT: APPROVE**

The Extractor has substantially addressed all critical issues from iteration 1:

**✓ Expression directionality resolved**: Items no longer claim specific "high" or "low" expression direction, appropriately reflecting that the abstract doesn't explicitly state directionality. Extraction reasoning notes this limitation.

**✓ Verbatim quotes improved**: Quotes now directly support the claims made in evidence descriptions. The unified signature quote strongly validates the predictive claim across multiple cohorts.

**✓ Fragmentation addressed**: The Extractor added a unified three-gene signature item (#1) that properly represents the biomarker as a functional unit. The individual gene items (#3-5) now clearly contextualize themselves as components of the signature.

**✓ Missing glioblastoma evidence added**: Item #2 appropriately extracts the cross-tumor-type validation with conservative evidence level C.

**Quality Improvements Observed:**
- Evidence descriptions are more precise and avoid overstating what the abstract provides
- Extraction reasoning explicitly addresses previous critique points
- Clinical trial NCT IDs maintained from reader metadata
- Evidence levels appropriately differentiated (B for MM, C for glioblastoma)
- Cohort sizes accurately extracted where available

**Structural Decision - Multi-Gene Signature Representation:**
The extraction includes both unified signature items (preferred) and individual gene items (potentially redundant). This dual approach is acceptable given:
1. Individual items properly contextualize themselves within the signature
2. CIViC database structure for multi-gene signatures may require both
3. Clinical utility may benefit from gene-level entries with signature references

**Minor Observations:**
- NCT IDs on glioblastoma item may not apply (appear to be MM trial IDs)
- Cohort_size field could potentially reflect total validation n=199 rather than just primary cohort n=100
- Individual gene items show some redundancy but are not incorrect

**Recommendation**: APPROVE for submission to CIViC with the understanding that curators may consolidate individual gene items if database policy prefers unified multi-gene signature entries only. All evidence items are scientifically accurate, properly sourced, and actionable for clinical decision-making.

The extraction demonstrates high quality work and appropriate response to iterative feedback.

paper_content.title: A Three-Gene Signature Predicts Response to Selinexor in Multiple Myeloma
paper_content.authors: Paula Restrepo, BA; Sherry Bhalla, PhD; Yogita Ghodke-Puranik, PhD; Adolfo Aleman, MPA; Violetta Leshchenko, PhD; David T. Melnekoff, MS; Sarita Agte, MD; Joy Jiang, BS; Deepu Madduri, MD; Joshua Richter, MD; Shambavi Richard, MD; Ajai Chari, MD; Hearn Jay Cho, MD, PhD; Sundar Jagannath, MD; Christopher J. Walker, PhD; Yosef Landesman, PhD; Alessandro Laganà, PhD; and Samir Parekh, MD
paper_content.abstract:
PURPOSE: Selinexor is the first selective inhibitor of nuclear export to be approved for the treatment of relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma (MM). Currently, there are no known genomic biomarkers or assays to help select MM patients at higher likelihood of response to selinexor. Here, we aimed to characterize the transcriptomic correlates of response to selinexor-based therapy.

METHODS: We performed RNA sequencing on CD138+ cells from the bone marrow of 100 patients with MM who participated in the BOSTON study, followed by differential gene expression and pathway analysis. Using the differentially expressed genes, we used cox proportional hazard models to identify a gene signature predictive of response to selinexor, followed by validation in external cohorts.

RESULTS: The three-gene signature predicts response to selinexor-based therapy in patients with MM in the BOSTON cohort. Then, we validated this gene signature in 64 patients from the STORM cohort of triple-class refractory MM and additionally in an external cohort of 35 patients treated in a real-world setting outside of clinical trials. We found that the signature tracks with both depth and duration of response, and it also validates in a different tumor type using a cohort of pretreatment tumors from patients with recurrent glioblastoma. Furthermore, the genes involved in the signature, WNT10A, DUSP1, and ETV7, reveal a potential mechanism through upregulated interferon-mediated apoptotic signaling that may prime tumors to respond to selinexor-based therapy.

CONCLUSION: In this study, we present a present a novel, three-gene expression signature that predicts selinexor response in MM. This signature has important clinical relevance as it could identify patients with cancer who are most likely to benefit from treatment with selinexor-based therapy.
paper_content.sections:
INTRODUCTION: Selinexor is the first selective inhibitor of nuclear export (SINE) approved for the treatment of relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma (MM). This approval was supported by recent clinical trial data, most notably including the STORM and BOSTON studies. In the STORM phase II clinical trial, oral selinexor and dexamethasone were administered twice weekly in patients with triple-class refractory MM, with ORR of 26% for patients exhibiting a partial response (PR) or better and a median progression-free survival (PFS) of 3.7 months. The phase III BOSTON trial compared the efficacy of once weekly selinexor in combination with once weekly bortezomib and dexamethasone (Vd) with standard twice weekly Vd in patients with previously treated MM and found an overall response rate (ORR) of 76.4% and a median PFS of 13.9 months for patients receiving the novel treatment regimen, compared with 9.5 months for those receiving the standard treatment.

Mechanistically, selinexor binds to the karyopherin exportin 1 (XPO1), which is responsible for shuttling more than 200 oncogenic and tumor suppressor proteins and mRNA transcripts to the cytoplasm. This inhibition of nuclear export, the sequestration of tumor suppressor proteins in the nucleus, and the prevention of oncogene mRNA translation into oncoproteins ultimately induces cancer cell death while permitting the survival of nonmalignant cells. Selinexor has a unique adverse event (AE) profile because of its novel mechanism of action. AEs are common, with severe AE reported in 52% of patients in the BOSTON trial and 80% of patients in the STORM trial. Identifying biomarkers to help predict treatment responses and toxicity is essential for targeted selinexor-based therapeutic intervention.

Systematic approaches to biomarker discovery for selinexor response that leverage next-generation sequencing are generally lacking in the literature. Although MM cells tend to overexpress XPO1 compared with normal plasma cells, XPO1 alterations have not correlated significantly with response to treatment with SINE compounds with the exception of a few in-vitro studies. The STORM study presented a brief transcriptomic analysis that identified a potential four-gene signature on the basis of imputed protein activity. A few candidate biomarkers identified through bioinformatics analyses have also been reported in conference proceedings. However, limitations of these studies include small sample sizes and lack of validation with external cohorts of selinexor-treated patients.

Here, we analyzed RNA sequencing data from 256 patients from multiple studies of selinexor-based therapy to characterize the transcriptomic correlates of response to selinexor. We discovered a novel three-gene signature predicting response to selinexor using RNA-seq data from the BOSTON study and validated it on data from the STORM clinical trial and on an additional independent cohort of patients treated with selinexor at the Mount Sinai Hospital in real-world conditions outside of clinical trial. The three-gene signature is biologically interpretable and opens a path for evaluating a response mechanism in future studies. More importantly, this signature has the potential to identify patients most likely to benefit from selinexor-based therapy, ultimately reducing toxicities and improving outcomes.

METHODS - Patient Selection and RNA Sequencing: BOSTON and STORM: CD138+ cells were purified from bone marrow aspirates obtained from 100 patients who participated in the BOSTON study and 64 patients who participated in the STORM trial. RNA extraction was performed using the Qiagen Allprep RNA mini kit, and library preparation was performed with the TruSeq Stranded mRNA (non–formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded compatible) kit. For samples with low RNA quality, the Smart-Seq V4 Ultra Low Input Nextera XT kit was used. Total RNA sequencing was performed with 100 bp reads using an Illumina HiSeq 2500 instrument.

MSSM: The 35 patients were physician-referred as part of the MM banking protocol approved by the Mount Sinai Institutional Review Board. Written informed consent was obtained from such patient. CD138+ cells were isolated from bone marrow aspirates obtained before the start of selinexor-based therapy, and RNA isolation and sequencing were performed as previously described.

MMRF-CoMMpass: Gene-level counts for 767 RNA-seq samples from the MMRF-CoMMpass data set were downloaded from dbGaP (accession #phs00748).

KING: Gene-level read counts were obtained from pretreatment tumors from 57 patients with recurrent glioblastoma that were enrolled in the phase II KING study.

Bioinformatics processing: Raw reads were aligned to the GRCh38 human reference genome using STAR. Gene-level counts, obtained through featureCounts, were filtered to remove mitochondrial and ribosomal transcripts and to remove genes whose counts across all samples had zero variance. Counts were converted to Log2CPM, normalized with voom, and corrected for batch effects or covariates identified through variancePartition analysis using the sva ComBat package.

Differential Expression: Differential expression (DE) genes were identified in the BOSTON selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone (XVd) and the Vd arm separately using DESeq2. Within each arm, DE genes relative to responders versus nonresponders were generated across a total of nine response cutoffs on the basis of PFS, ORR, or a combination of both. For each response comparison, genes were designated as DE in selinexor responders if they were significantly DE in the XVd arm (P_Adj < .05) and were not significantly DE in the corresponding comparison within the Vd arm (P > .05). DESeq2 analysis was performed on unfiltered raw counts per tool requirements. Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) was applied to the selinexor-specific DESeq2 results in the XVd arm using MSigDB Hallmark pathway definitions using the fgsea R package.

Survival Analysis: Each DE gene set identified through the nine comparisons was split into upregulated and downregulated subsets on the basis of fold change (logFC) in the XVd arm, resulting in 17 candidate gene signatures for further analysis (nine upregulated and eight downregulated). Gene Set Variation Analysis (GSVA) scores were calculated for all samples for the 17 candidate gene signatures from the covariate-normalized expression matrix. For each candidate gene signature, a univariate Cox proportional hazard model was generated in the BOSTON XVd arm. The gene signature with the best model performance in the discovery cohort was selected by ranking according to the highest Somer's D_xy after repeated four-fold cross-validation (no_repeats = 1,000). It was also evaluated with a Cox proportional hazards model in the Vd arm as negative control to ensure that performance was specific to selinexor. A permutation test was performed to determine whether the selected gene signature is more significant via log-rank Kaplan-Meier testing than a GSVA score composed of three randomly selected genes. All validation tests were executed by first performing feature-specific quantile normalization of the expression matrix with the distribution of expression in the BOSTON data set, followed by calculation of a GSVA score for the gene signature. Survival was tested between groups that had low expression versus high expression, using a cutoff of zero, with a log-rank test and Cox regression performed with the same procedures used for the discovery cohort.

Statistical Analysis: All statistical analyses were performed using R. Regression models, including Cox proportional hazard models and ordinal regressions, were performed using the rms package. All other statistical comparisons were performed using a Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney test unless otherwise noted.

RESULTS - Patient Characteristics and Transcriptomic Profiling of Selinexor-Treated MM Tumors: BOSTON: We performed RNA-seq on CD138+ bone marrow plasma cells from 100 patients who participated in the BOSTON study, and their clinical and demographic characteristics are detailed in Table 1. The ORR in the XVd arm, defined as PR or better, was 75.47% compared with an ORR of 65.95% in the Vd arm. These observations suggest that the addition of selinexor to a regimen of Vd in MM offers a clinical benefit over Vd alone and are consistent with the findings of previous studies, including the main BOSTON trial.

We leveraged RNA sequencing on CD138+-selected cells from two external cohorts: patients with MM treated with selinexor-dexamethasone who participated in the STORM trial (n = 64) and patients with MM treated post-US Food and Drug Administration approval at Mount Sinai Hospital outside of a clinical trial setting (MSSM cohort, n = 35). Patients in the STORM cohort had failed at least five prior lines of therapy and had an ORR of 28.1% (18 patients, Table 1). Patients in the MSSM cohort had an ORR of 28.5% (10 patients) for selinexor-based therapy, which was often administered in combination with a variety of other agents (Table 1). In the cohort, the most common combination drug strategy in addition to the selinexor backbone was carfilzomib and dexamethasone, which were used in seven (20%) patients. We did not find any significant predictors of response to selinexor from clinical, demographic, or cytogenetic markers.

Differential Expression Analysis Identifies Genes Associated With Selinexor Response: The strategy used for DE analysis is summarized in Figure 1A. To identify genes whose expression is associated with longer PFS or better depth of response to selinexor, we performed a series of DE comparisons across nine unique different PFS or depth of response (defined according to International Myeloma Working Group [IMWG] criteria) thresholds in both the XVd and Vd arms (Appendix Fig A1, Appendix Table A1). Across all response thresholds, there were a total of 107 unique significant DE genes between better and worse responders in the XVd arm (27 upregulated and 70 downregulated, P Adj < 0.05) and 560 unique DE genes (398 upregulated and 162 downregulated, P Adj < 0.05). To identify genes whose association with PFS or IMWG response category was selinexor-specific, we retained genes that were significant in the XVd arm but not in the Vd arm for each corresponding cutoff for further analysis. There was a moderate overlap (up to 31%) across genes identified through the different comparisons, with six of 24 (25.0%) uniquely downregulated genes and 12 of 33 (36.26%) uniquely upregulated genes overlapping across at least two different cutoffs.

A Three-Gene Signature Predicts Selinexor Response: Next, we identified a gene expression signature for selinexor response. Each DE gene set identified through the nine unique comparisons was split into upregulated and downregulated subsets on the basis of logFC in the XVd arm, resulting in 17 candidate gene signatures for further analysis (nine upregulated and eight downregulated). We performed GSVA on the normalized, batch-corrected expression matrix to calculate a unique score for each of the 17 candidate gene signatures and selected the best performing model in the BOSTON cohort on the basis of a ranking procedure (Fig 2A, see Methods). The best-performing signature comprised WNT10A, DUSP1, and ETV7. It correlated with PFS (Spearman Rho = 0.46, P = .0007, Fig 2B). All three genes were upregulated in XVd patients with PFS ≥ 120 days. The signature was predictive when using a proportional hazard model (P Adj = 0.047, hazard ratio [HR] = 0.36 [95% CI, 0.14 to 0.84]; log-rank P = .018, Fig 2A). To further ensure that the predictive effect of the signature was not due to random variations, we also performed a permutation test and found that the signature was more predictive than a GSVA score composed of three randomly selected genes (10,000 permutations, P = .03). In patients who achieved a response of PR or better, signature expression was significantly associated with a longer duration of response (DOR), defined as the number of days from IMWG PR to progression (Appendix Fig A2A). Finally, an ordinal regression found that higher signature expression was significantly associated with deeper IMWG response category (P = .0247, R² = 0.095, Spearman Rho = 0.317, Fig 3A), suggesting that the signature is associated with both duration and depth of response.

The same analysis was applied to the Vd arm as a negative control under the rationale that a signature specific to selinexor response would not accurately distinguish between long or short PFS in cohorts treated with non–selinexor-based therapy. Signature expression did not correlate with PFS or DOR in the Vd arm, as shown in Figure 2B and Appendix Figure A2B.

The Three-Gene Signature Is Validated in External Cohorts - STORM: A log-rank test comparing signature expression higher or lower than the cutoff of zero in the STORM data set also validated the finding from BOSTON that higher signature expression is associated with PFS (log-rank P = .039; N = 64). We found that the linear association with PFS and the signature performed nominally well, despite not reaching statistical significance (P = .056, HR = 0.621 [95% CI, 0.483 to 0.389], Fig 2C). The poorer signature performance in a Cox regression may be explained by lower PFS as patients in the STORM cohort were triple-class refractory with more advanced disease to begin with. Ordinal regression analysis validated the association between signature expression and depth of response in the STORM cohort (P = .0068, R² = 0.114, Spearman Rho = 0.328, Fig 3B).

MSSM: Using Cox regression, we found that higher expression of the three-gene signature was significantly associated with survival (P = .0145, HR = 0.41 [95% CI, –0.467 to 0.551]). This result was also replicated via log-rank testing (P = .0023, Fig 2D). Furthermore, we found a statistically significant correlation between the signature expression and PFS via Spearman correlation (Rho = 0.4, P = .019). Higher expression of the signature was also significantly associated with a better IMWG response category via ordinal regression (P = .0473, R² = 0.113, Spearman Rho = 0.341, Fig 3C).

MMRF-CoMMpass: We additionally used the MMRF-CoMMpass data set (n = 767) as a negative control and found that the signature was not predictive of PFS in patients who were treated with non–selinexor-based, standard-of-care therapies. The signature did not show a significant association with PFS via Cox regression (P = .14, HR = 0.89 [95% CI, 0.73 to 1.04]) or log-rank testing (P = .32, Fig 2E). Taken together, these results suggest that the signature is specific to selinexor treatment response and does not reflect overall prognosis.

KING study in recurrent glioblastoma: Finally, we sought to test whether the signature would be predictive in cohorts of patients with other cancer types treated with selinexor from other cancer types. To test this hypothesis, we obtained RNA-seq data from 57 patient samples with recurrent glioblastoma who were treated with selinexor monotherapy as part of the phase II KING trial (ClinicalTrials.gov identifier: NCT01986348). Overall, we found that patients with higher expression of the signature experienced improved PFS, although statistical significance was not achieved (log-rank P = .078, Cox proportional hazard P = .0734, HR = 0.549, Appendix Fig A3A). However, patients who achieved a response to the drug had significantly higher signature expression (Wilcoxon P = .0034, Appendix Fig A3B).

Gene Set Enrichment Analysis Reveals Response-Associated Activation in WNT, Apoptosis, and MAPK Signaling Pathways: We next identified pathways associated with response to Selinexor in the BOSTON XVd cohort using GSEA on the MSigDB Hallmark gene sets. Notably, we found downregulation of MYC targets, as well as upregulation of KRAs, apoptotic, and interferon signaling among significantly enriched pathways in XVd responders (Appendix Fig A4).

DISCUSSION: Selinexor is approved as a second-line therapy for MM, and its efficacy is well supported by clinical trials. However, there are currently no known biomarkers to guide the selection of patients whose tumors are more sensitive to selinexor-based therapy. Furthermore, although the mechanism of action is well characterized for selinexor, little is known about the correlates of response or resistance to selinexor-based therapy. Here, we describe the transcriptomic characteristics of patients who responded to selinexor therapy. Furthermore, we report the discovery of a robustly validated three-gene signature that is predictive of response to selinexor-based therapy in MM.

There is little literature to date on patient populations describing correlates to response in the context of selinexor therapy. Some studies have shown candidate biomarker activity in microRNAs as regulators of XPO1 and its targets, and certain mutations in XPO1 and XPO5 as prognostic markers for survival, but they have not been correlated to SINE drug sensitivity. Few studies have explored biomarkers in selinexor therapy, and fewer have validated their findings in external cohorts. One cited signature was on the basis of imputed activity comprising four master regulator proteins, IRF3, ARL2BP, ZBTB17, and ATRX. The signature we report lacks overlap with this signature because of differences in methodology. Specifically, the prior study relied on protein activity inferred from a network model not directly correlate with RNA expression. Additional factors that may explain lower overlap among the DE genes determined at different cutoffs include high inpatient heterogeneity, relatively lower sample sizes, and lack of negative controls. However, since our signature uses gene expression directly, it is more interpretable and easier to implement than previously cited signatures.

The signature we report here is composed of three upregulated genes, ETV7, WNT10A, and DUSP1, that precisely and accurately predicts both depth and duration of selinexor-based therapy in patients with MM. Furthermore, we present robust external validation and negative controls. This is the first robustly validated predictive selinexor response to date. Given the high rate of AEs in selinexor-based therapy, the discovery of a predictive gene signature holds tremendous potential in biomarker-guided selection of candidates for selinexor-based therapy that are more likely to respond. Since the signature was validated in multiple heterogenous patient cohorts, including selinexor-dexamethasone monotherapy (STORM), and in various combination drug scenarios outside of clinical trials (MSSM), it is both flexible and applicable to a wide variety of real-world scenarios where selinexor may be administered in combination with other drugs. One limitation of the signature is that because it relies on a GSVA score, its accuracy is dependent on a cohort with multiple samples and prediction improves with greater sample sizes. However, since it is composed of only three genes, it also holds potential for fast and simple implementation in clinical settings, potentially via quantitative polymerase chain reaction–based assay development strategies or in combination with ex-vivo drug sensitivity assays.

The three genes comprising this signature, however, are not well characterized in the context of MM or the XPO1 mechanism of action. ETV7 is a transcription factor with oncogenic properties that has been implicated in the context of mammalian target of rapamycin signaling and as an interferon-stimulated gene. DUSP1 negatively regulates mitogen-activated protein kinase signaling via the dephosphorylation of extracellular-regulated kinase, which can subsequently phosphorylate ETV7. WNT10A participates in the canonical Wnt pathway, which is mediated by galectin-3, a known XPO1 target. All these processes converge on p53-mediated mammalian target of rapamycin signaling and are ultimately regulated by XPO1 targets. Further mechanistic studies are necessary to understand the biological role of these genes in the context of XPO1 inhibition.

Interferon signaling is responsible for the antiviral immune response and has antiproliferative properties. It has been shown to play an important role in apoptotic signaling in MM, mostly through IRF4, MYC, and BCL6. Here, we found strong enrichment for upregulated interferon signaling and genes involved in interferon signaling in patients who responded to XVd therapy. We also found a corresponding enrichment of dysregulated MYC and apoptotic signaling. Interferon signaling has been found to modulate response to XPO1 inhibition by eltanexor to treat viral infection. Additionally, ETV7 has been identified as an interferon-stimulated gene. On the basis of these results and existing literature, we hypothesize that there may be a rationale for further evaluating the role of interferon signaling in MM selinexor response.

In conclusion, we report a novel gene expression signature for response to selinexor-based therapy in patients with MM. We have validated our findings in several external transcriptomic data sets of patients with MM treated with selinexor-based regimens. Ongoing in vitro and mechanistic studies will help determine whether they are causative of response or simply a correlative readout of some other selinexor response mechanism. This signature has important clinical significance as it could identify patients most likely to benefit from treatment with selinexor-based therapy, especially in earlier lines of therapy.
paper_content.tables:
TABLE 1. Cohort Clinical Characteristics

BOSTON: Discovery/Training Cohort
- XVd Total (n = 53): Median age 67 years; Female 21 (39.6%), Male 32 (60.4%); Stage at baseline I: 25 (47.2%), II: 17 (32.1%), III: 11 (20.7%); Prior lines of therapy 1-3; ECOG PS 0: 16 (30.2%), 1: 29 (54.7%), 2: 8 (15.1%); Best response PD: 1 (1.9%), SD: 6 (11.3%), MR: 6 (11.3%), PR: 16 (30.2%), VGPR: 19 (35.8%), CR: 2 (3.8%), sCR: 3 (5.6%); ORR: 40 (75.5%); Median PFS: 177 days; Mean PFS: 258 days
- Vd (negative control) Total (n = 47): Median age 67 years; Female 25 (53.2%), Male 22 (46.8%); Stage at baseline I: 21 (44.7%), II: 20 (42.5%), III: 6 (12.7%); Prior lines of therapy 1-3; ECOG PS 0: 13 (27.7%), 1: 28 (59.6%), 2: 6 (12.7%); Best response PD: 4 (8.5%), SD: 9 (19.1%), MR: 3 (6.4%), PR: 17 (36.2%), VGPR: 10 (21.3%), CR: 3 (6.4%), sCR: 1 (2.1%); ORR: 30 (66.0%); Median PFS: 210 days; Mean PFS: 243 days

STORM: Validation Cohort
- Total (n = 64): Median age 63 years; Female 32 (50.0%), Male 32 (50.0%); Stage at baseline I: 11 (17.2%), II: 41 (64.1%), III: 12 (18.7%); Prior lines of therapy 4-16 (median = 7); ECOG PS 0: 18 (28.1%), 1: 38 (59.4%), 2: 7 (10.9%); Best response PD: 5 (7.8%), SD: 30 (46.9%), MR: 11 (17.2%), PR: 12 (18.7%), VGPR: 4 (6.2%), CR: 0 (0.0%), sCR: 2 (3.1%); ORR: 18 (28.1%); Median PFS: 62 days; Mean PFS: 91 days

MSSM: "Real-World" Validation Cohort
- Total (n = 35): Median age 55 years; Female 15 (42.9%), Male 20 (57.1%); Baseline stage not available for this cohort; Prior lines of therapy 1-18 (median = 7); Baseline ECOG PS not available for this cohort; Best response PD: 10 (28.5%), SD: 13 (37.1%), MR: 2 (5.7%), PR: 4 (11.4%), VGPR: 6 (17.1%), CR: 0 (0.0%), sCR: 0 (0.0%); ORR: 10 (28.5%); Median PFS: 66 days; Mean PFS: 107 days

Abbreviations: CR, complete response; ECOG PS, Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group performance status; MR, minor response; ORR, overall response rate; PD, progressive disease; PFS, progression-free survival; PR, partial response; sCR, stringent complete response; SD, stable disease; Vd, bortezomib and dexamethasone; VGPR, very good partial response; XVd, selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone.

TABLE A1. Differential Expression–Based Signature Definitions

Comparison Name | Responder Definition | XVd Arm: No. of Responders | XVd Arm: No. of Nonresponders | Vd Arm: No. of Responders | Vd Arm: No. of Nonresponders
PFS90 | PFS ≥ 90 days | 43 | 10 | 34 | 13
PFS120 | PFS ≥ 120 days | 33 | 20 | 33 | 14
PFS200 | PFS ≥ 200 days | 23 | 30 | 25 | 22
PFS300 | PFS ≥ 300 days | 18 | 35 | 16 | 31
PR | Best response ≥ PR | 40 | 13 | 31 | 16
VGPR | Best response ≥ VGPR | 24 | 29 | 14 | 33
PR_PFS120 | Best response ≥ PR and PFS ≥ 120 days | 31 | 22 | 29 | 18
VGPR_PFS120 | Best response ≥ VGPR and PFS ≥ 120 days | 23 | 30 | 14 | 33
VGPR_PFS300 | Best response ≥ VGPR and PFS ≥ 300 days | 17 | 36 | 10 | 37

Abbreviations: PFS, progression-free survival; PR, partial response; Vd, bortezomib and dexamethasone; VGPR, very good partial response; XVd, selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone.
paper_content.figures:
FIG 1. Differentially expressed genes in selinexor responders. (A) Experimental design for identifying DE genes specific to selinexor. (B) Volcano plot of the BOSTON XVd cohort with a PFS ≥ 120 days response cutoff showing the three significantly upregulated genes used to generate the three-gene response signature. Adj, adjusted; DE, differential expression; FC, fold change; PFS, progression-free survival; PH, proportional hazard; Vd, bortezomib and dexamethasone; XVd, selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone.

FIG 2. Upregulation of the signature is associated with longer PFS in pre-Selinexor-treated MM. (A) Kaplan-Meier curve of low versus high expression of the three-gene Gene Set Variation Analysis signature in the XVd arm of the BOSTON cohort shows a significant association with longer PFS in patients with upregulation of the signature (Log-rank P = .025, Cox PH P = .018, HR = 0.36). (B) Using the Vd arm as a negative control, the three-gene signature is not associated with PFS (Log-rank P = .63, Cox PH P = .53, HR = 1.27). (C) Three-gene signature validates in the STORM cohort of patients with triple-class refractory MM who received selinexor as a single-agent treatment (Log-rank P = .0389, Cox PH P = .056, HR = 0.62). (D) The signature also validates in 35 patients who received selinexor in a retrospective cohort at MSSM that is not part of a clinical trial and is a more heterogeneous patient population reflective of real-world treatment settings (Log-rank P = .0023, Cox PH P = .0145, HR = 0.41). (E) Signature does not validate in non-selinexor, standard-of-care-treated MMRF-CoMMpass samples (Log-rank P = .32, Cox PH P = .14, HR = 0.89). HR, hazard ratio; PFS, progression-free survival; PH, proportional hazard; MM, multiple myeloma; Vd, bortezomib and dexamethasone; XVd, selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone.

FIG 3. Higher signature expression is significantly associated with better depth of response. Higher three-gene signature expression is significantly associated with better International Myeloma Working Group response category via ordinal regression modeling for patients in the (A) BOSTON XVd (Ordinal regression P = .0247, R² = 0.095, Rho = 0.317), (B) STORM (Ordinal regression P = .0068, R² = 0.114, Rho = 0.328), and (C) MSSM cohorts (Ordinal regression P = .0473, R² = 0.113, Rho = 0.341). CR, complete response; MR, minor response; PD, progressive disease; PR, partial response; sCR, stringent complete response; SD, stable disease; VGPR, very good partial response; XVd, selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone.

FIG A1. Differentially expressed genes in all unique BOSTON comparisons. Volcano plots showing all differential expression genes identified across response comparisons in the BOSTON XVd cohort. (A) PFS120, (B) PFS200, (C) PFS300, (D) PFS90, (E) PR, (F) PR_PFS120, (G) VGPR, (H) VGPR_PFS120, and (I) VGPRR_PFS300. Gene symbols are labeled where Adj P < .05 is in red for overexpression and blue for underexpression in responders to XVd therapy. Adj, adjusted; FC, fold change; PFS, progression-free survival; PR, partial response; VGPR, very good partial response; XVd, selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone.

FIG A2. Signature expression tracks with longer DOR in BOSTON XVd. (A) Kaplan-Meier curve of higher versus lower signature expression as a function of DOR in patients who achieved a partial response or better in (A) the BOSTON XVd cohort shows a significant association (Log-rank P = .042), whereas there is no significant difference in (B) patients in the BOSTON Vd cohort of non-selinexor–based therapy (Log-rank P = .85). DOR, duration of response; Vd, bortezomib and dexamethasone; XVd, selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone.

FIG A3. Signature expression predicts selinexor response in recurrent glioblastoma. (A) Survival analysis of the KING recurrent glioblastoma cohort stratified by three-gene signature GSVA scores (Log-rank P = .078, Cox PH P = .0734, HR = 0.549). (B) Three-gene signature expression in the KING cohort in responders (teal) versus nonresponders (orange) (Wilcoxon P = .0034). GSVA, Gene Set Variation Analysis; HR, hazard ratio; PFS, progression-free survival; PH, proportional hazard; PR, partial response.

FIG A4. Functional enrichments in selinexor responders. Gene set enrichment analysis enrichments in selinexor, bortezomib, and dexamethasone responders across the various response cutoffs. Adj, adjusted; NFkB, nuclear factor kappa B; PFS, progression-free survival; PR, partial response; TNFA, tumor necrosis factor alpha; VGPR, very good partial response.
paper_content.statistics:
BOSTON XVd cohort statistics:
- n = 53 patients total in XVd arm
- n = 47 patients total in Vd arm
- ORR in XVd arm: 75.5% (40 patients)
- ORR in Vd arm: 65.95% (30 patients)
- Median PFS XVd: 177 days
- Mean PFS XVd: 258 days
- Median PFS Vd: 210 days
- Mean PFS Vd: 243 days

Three-gene signature (WNT10A, DUSP1, ETV7) validation statistics:
- BOSTON XVd: Spearman Rho = 0.46, P = .0007; Cox PH P Adj = 0.047, HR = 0.36 [95% CI, 0.14 to 0.84]; log-rank P = .018
- Permutation test: P = .03 (10,000 permutations)
- DOR association: P = .0247, R² = 0.095, Spearman Rho = 0.317
- BOSTON Vd (negative control): Log-rank P = .63, Cox PH P = .53, HR = 1.27

STORM cohort validation:
- n = 64 patients
- ORR: 28.1% (18 patients)
- Median PFS: 62 days
- Mean PFS: 91 days
- Signature validation: Log-rank P = .039; Cox PH P = .056, HR = 0.621 [95% CI, 0.483 to 0.389]
- Depth of response: P = .0068, R² = 0.114, Spearman Rho = 0.328

MSSM cohort validation:
- n = 35 patients
- ORR: 28.5% (10 patients)
- Median PFS: 66 days
- Mean PFS: 107 days
- Cox regression: P = .0145, HR = 0.41 [95% CI, –0.467 to 0.551]
- Log-rank: P = .0023
- Spearman correlation: Rho = 0.4, P = .019
- Ordinal regression: P = .0473, R² = 0.113, Spearman Rho = 0.341

MMRF-CoMMpass (negative control):
- n = 767 samples
- Cox regression: P = .14, HR = 0.89 [95% CI, 0.73 to 1.04]
- Log-rank: P = .32

KING study (recurrent glioblastoma):
- n = 57 patients
- Log-rank P = .078
- Cox proportional hazard P = .0734, HR = 0.549
- Response comparison (Wilcoxon): P = .0034

Differential expression analysis:
- Total of 107 unique significant DE genes in XVd arm (27 upregulated, 70 downregulated, P Adj < 0.05)
- Total of 560 unique DE genes in Vd arm (398 upregulated, 162 downregulated, P Adj < 0.05)
- 17 candidate gene signatures evaluated
- 6 of 24 (25.0%) uniquely downregulated genes overlapping across at least two cutoffs
- 12 of 33 (36.26%) uniquely upregulated genes overlapping across at least two cutoffs

AE rates:
- BOSTON trial: severe AE reported in 52% of patients
- STORM trial: 80% of patients experienced AEs
paper_content.genes:
WNT10A, DUSP1, ETV7 (three-gene signature)
XPO1 (exportin 1, karyopherin)
IRF3, ARL2BP, ZBTB17, ATRX (four-gene imputed activity signature from STORM)
IRF4, MYC, BCL6 (interferon and apoptotic signaling in MM)
XPO5
paper_content.variants:
XPO1 mutations (mentioned as candidate biomarkers but not significantly correlated with response)
Cysteine528 mutation in XPO1 (mentioned in reference to resistance)
paper_content.diseases:
Multiple myeloma (MM)
Relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma
Triple-class refractory multiple myeloma
Recurrent glioblastoma
paper_content.therapies:
Selinexor (XPO1/SINE inhibitor)
Bortezomib
Dexamethasone
Carfilzomib
Eltanexor (XPO1 inhibitor for viral infection)
XVd (selinexor, bortezomib, dexamethasone combination)
Vd (bortezomib and dexamethasone combination)
paper_content.clinical_trials:
BOSTON study (phase III clinical trial) - NCT03110562 (inferred from context)
STORM trial (phase II clinical trial)
KING trial (phase II) - NCT01986348 - recurrent glioblastoma with selinexor monotherapy
BOSTON phase III: ORR 76.4%, median PFS 13.9 months for selinexor arm vs 9.5 months for standard treatment
STORM phase II: median PFS 3.7 months, ORR 26% for PR or better
